question: FISA Section 702 reauthorized before it expires?

fragility_points: A narrower-than-written market interpretation could still create settlement risk.
Lack of direct Congress.gov access leaves a small unresolved source-of-truth gap.
key_assumptions: Public Law 118-49 counts exactly as the contract says.
Current codification accurately reflects that Section 702 remained authorized after the 2024 law.
main_logical_chain: A cold base rate for future congressional reauthorization alone would not justify extreme confidence.
But the contract explicitly names Public Law 118-49 as qualifying legislation.
Official law text shows that law was enacted in 2024 and amended Section 702.
Current codification supports that Section 702 remained in force after that enactment.
Therefore the event looks more like an already-satisfied settlement question than a fresh 2026 legislative race.
payload.main_thesis: The contract appears to be mostly already satisfied because it explicitly includes Public Law 118-49, which official law text shows was enacted in 2024 and reauthorized Section 702 before the market deadline.
payload.source_quality_view: Source quality is strong because the core evidence comes from official enacted-law and enrolled-bill text, with current codification used as an additional legal verification layer, though the named Congress.gov source could not be directly checked.
strongest_disconfirmers: The main residual risk is contract-interpretation risk if Public Law 118-49 were somehow excluded despite being named.
The exact Congress.gov tracker page named in the rules could not be directly checked due to anti-bot blocking.
strongest_supports: The market text explicitly says qualifying legislation includes Public Law 118-49.
Official govinfo law text shows Public Law 118-49 became law on April 20, 2024 and amended Section 702.
Current codification of 50 U.S.C. 1881a is consistent with continued Section 702 authority.
payload.timing_relevance: The relevant market deadline is April 19, 2026 at 11:59 PM ET, while the qualifying law identified here was enacted on April 20, 2024, well before that window closes.
unresolved_ambiguities: Whether Congress.gov would present any materially different sunset framing than the sources used here.
Whether market operators could apply a narrower reading than the quoted contract text.
payload.what_would_change_view: A direct Congress.gov check or market clarification excluding Public Law 118-49 would move the estimate down; direct Congress.gov confirmation that it counts would move confidence slightly higher.

fragility_points: No visible bill vehicle or floor schedule as the deadline approaches.
A Senate or House blocking coalition against a clean/near-clean extension.
Need for a more complex reform bargain than the calendar can support.
key_assumptions: If Congress acts in time, it will likely use a clean or near-clean extension vehicle rather than reopen the full reform fight.
Leadership can still coordinate bicameral passage before the deadline.
No late procedural blockade or new compliance scandal derails the extension path.
main_logical_chain: The market is pricing a high probability largely because 702 is an important national-security authority.
But this contract is deadline-sensitive and requires enacted law, not just broad support.
An official March 19 statement provides a concrete late-stage catalyst path via an 18-month clean extension.
That keeps Yes above 50%, but ongoing coalition and timing risk make 78.5% look too high.
payload.main_thesis: Section 702 is slightly more likely than not to be reauthorized before expiry, but only through a late leadership-driven extension path and with meaningful deadline fragility.
quote_anchors: The President is seeking an 18-month clean extension.
the path for reauthorization is unclear
payload.source_quality_view: Source quality is medium-to-high overall: one direct official catalyst signal plus strong contextual analysis, but incomplete direct bill-tracker access lowers confidence somewhat.
strongest_disconfirmers: The contract requires bicameral passage and enactment by a strict deadline, so timing slippage alone can resolve No.
Brookings described the path as politically unclear and difficult because reform fights remain unresolved.
strongest_supports: House Intelligence Chair Crawford said on March 19 that the President sought an 18-month clean extension and that he was working with leadership and Judiciary to support it.
Congress has a structural incentive not to let a core intelligence authority lapse outright.
payload.timing_relevance: The key repricing catalyst is visible movement toward an enactable legislative vehicle; absent that, the strict deadline should pressure the price lower.
unresolved_ambiguities: Whether the qualifying vehicle will actually be the named Congress.gov bill or another legislative path.
How much real bicameral support exists right now versus general elite preference for eventual renewal.
payload.what_would_change_view: I would move higher on confirmed bill movement or bicameral floor commitments, and lower if no vehicle emerges soon or a blocking coalition becomes explicit.

main_logical_chain: Public Law 118-49 is official enacted law dated Apr. 20, 2024.
That law appears to move the operative sunset to two years after enactment, i.e. Apr. 20, 2026.
The market resolves on enactment by Apr. 19, 2026 11:59 PM ET.
If real legal urgency arrives after the market deadline, slippage risk is underpriced.
Therefore Yes is still likelier than No, but below the market's 78.5% implied probability.
